subtopic_detail: Empiric antibiotic therapy for suspected infective endocarditis in an IVDU
stem: A 32-year-old man with active intravenous heroin use presents with 5 days of fevers, rigors and pleuritic chest pain. He is haemodynamically stable but unwell. Three sets of peripheral blood cultures have been drawn from separate sites. Bedside transthoracic echo shows a 9 mm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with mild tricuspid regurgitation. Chest imaging shows multiple peripheral nodular opacities consistent with septic pulmonary emboli.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68, HR 108, RR 22, SpO2 94% on room air, Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Multiple needle track marks both antecubital fossae; soft systolic murmur at lower left sternal edge increasing on inspiration; no peripheral stigmata of left-sided IE
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, CRP 184 mg/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L; troponin negative; ECG sinus tachycardia

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin with intravenous gentamicin empirically while cultures are pending, with daily levels and renal function checks
options.rationale: AU empiric therapy for suspected IE in an IVDU covers methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus with vancomycin and adds gentamicin for synergy until cultures and susceptibilities return. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily as monotherapy, given its broad gram-negative cover and convenient once-daily administration in this presentation
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not reliably cover MRSA, the dominant pathogen in IVDU-associated right-sided IE. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Defer all antibiotics for 48 hours to allow blood cultures to declare a pathogen before any empirical antibiotic exposure
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a febrile patient with vegetations and septic emboli risks progression; empirical cover is started after cultures are drawn. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily with outpatient cardiology follow-up arranged for the following week
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inadequate for the induction phase of IE; intravenous therapy in hospital is required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g six-hourly as a single agent based on streptococcal cover for community-acquired endocarditis
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone misses MRSA and Staphylococcus aureus, the leading IVDU IE pathogens. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for suspected infective endocarditis in an IVDU covers Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA with vancomycin, with gentamicin added for synergy and gram-negative cover until cultures return. Three sets of cultures from separate venepunctures precede antibiotics where time allows. Transthoracic echo identifies right-sided vegetations; transoesophageal echo is indicated if TTE is non-diagnostic or left-sided IE is suspected. Surgical referral applies to large vegetations, heart failure or refractory sepsis.
key_points: IVDU-associated right-sided IE is most commonly Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA
Empiric IV vancomycin plus gentamicin covers MRSA and provides synergy until cultures return
Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites precede antibiotics where the clinical state allows
explanation.pearls: Septic pulmonary emboli with a tricuspid vegetation in an IVDU points to Staphylococcus aureus; start vancomycin with gentamicin once cultures are drawn.

subtopic_detail: Post-exposure prophylaxis after occupational HIV needlestick
stem: A 29-year-old surgical resident sustains a deep hollow-needle injury during arterial line placement in a known HIV-positive patient with an unsuppressed viral load on adherence-poor antiretrovirals. The injury was 40 minutes ago. The resident is healthy, on no medications and not pregnant. She has had a single hepatitis B vaccination course with documented anti-HBs above 100 mIU/mL six months ago. Her last hepatitis C and HIV screens at occupational health 3 months ago were negative.

data_table.Examination: Wound at left index finger pulp, bleeding milked and wound irrigated with saline
data_table.Investigations: Source patient HIV RNA above 100,000 copies/mL on last clinic visit; source HCV antibody negative, HBsAg negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send source patient and worker baseline HIV serology and arrange to start any prophylaxis in the morning once the laboratory has reported results
options.rationale: PEP efficacy declines steeply beyond 24 hours; starting prophylaxis cannot wait for baseline serology when the source is high-risk. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Commence tenofovir disoproxil with emtricitabine and dolutegravir as a 28-day three-drug course now and arrange baseline and follow-up testing in parallel
options.rationale: High-risk occupational exposure to a high viral load source mandates three-drug PEP started as soon as possible, ideally within hours, for 28 days. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Give a single 50 mg dose of dolutegravir as a pragmatic monotherapy prophylaxis until source results return at the next clinic visit
options.rationale: Single-dose monotherapy is not endorsed PEP; three-drug regimens for 28 days are the standard. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Test only the exposed healthcare worker now and defer any prophylaxis until her own baseline serology is available the following day
options.rationale: Worker baseline serology is essential but does not delay PEP initiation in a high-risk exposure. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the worker that her hepatitis B vaccine response covers her, and arrange follow-up serology at 3 months without any antiretroviral therapy
options.rationale: Hepatitis B immunity does not protect against HIV; PEP is required for a needlestick from a viraemic HIV source. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: High-risk occupational HIV exposure (deep hollow-needle injury from a viraemic source) mandates three-drug PEP started as soon as possible, ideally within hours and within 72 hours, for 28 days. The Australian first-line regimen is tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine with dolutegravir or raltegravir. Baseline HIV serology is taken in parallel and repeated at 6 weeks, 3 months and (if indicated) 6 months. Hepatitis B and C status of source and worker are also documented.
key_points: Three-drug PEP within 72 hours, ideally within hours, of high-risk HIV exposure
First-line AU regimen: tenofovir disoproxil with emtricitabine plus dolutegravir for 28 days
Follow-up HIV serology at 6 weeks and 3 months, with earlier symptomatic testing if seroconversion is suspected
explanation.pearls: PEP started within 4 hours and continued for 28 days reduces the risk of HIV seroconversion after needlestick by around 80 percent; tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir is the AU preferred regimen.

sources.label: Australian Society for Infectious Diseases occupational exposure statement
